I am honored to nominate [NOMINEE] for Nurse of the Year in recognition of her exceptional dedication to delivering safe, seamless, and compassionate care—particularly for patients with complex medical, behavioral health, and social needs. In the high-acuity environment of the Stroger Emergency Department, [NOMINEE] consistently goes beyond traditional nursing roles to ensure that care transitions are not only clinically appropriate, but also trauma-informed and responsive to the lived realities of our most vulnerable patients.

[NOMINEE] demonstrates a deep understanding of how behavioral health challenges, homelessness, and systemic barriers intersect to drive poor outcomes and avoidable readmissions. She actively works to mitigate these risks by advocating for patients, addressing social determinants of health, and navigating complex discharge barriers with creativity and persistence. Her efforts directly contribute to improved patient satisfaction, continuity of care, and reduced recidivism.

One powerful example involved a patient experiencing homelessness who required continuous oxygen therapy. After being discharged from the ED, the patient faced a critical barrier: he could not receive a replacement oxygen tank because he lacked a stable address for delivery, and his current tank was nearly depleted. Recognizing both the immediate medical risk and the broader trauma associated with housing instability, [NOMINEE] took swift and compassionate action. She personally arranged and paid for a hotel stay to provide the patient with a safe, temporary environment where the oxygen delivery could occur. As a result, the patient received the necessary equipment, avoided a likely readmission, and experienced a moment of dignity, stability, and care that extended beyond the clinical encounter.

[NOMINEE]’s proactive and solution-oriented approach reflects her commitment to patient safety, equity, and whole-person care. She understands that timely resolution of system issues—including those related to health information systems—is critical to patient outcomes, and she does not hesitate to escalate concerns or seek support when needed. Her strong communication and collaboration across disciplines further strengthen care coordination and team effectiveness.

[NOMINEE] exemplifies the principles of trauma-informed, patient-centered care. Her reliability, critical thinking, compassion, and willingness to act in the face of complex challenges make her an outstanding nurse and a truly deserving candidate for Nurse of the Year.

[NOMINEE] is an exceptional team player regularly assisting her co-workers with nursing expertise. She does and excellent job working with ambulatory patient that have very high needs. For example, she was very effective in assisting a rising risk patient to significantly reduce their blood sugar level. Case Summary. Patient is a 59-year-old female with a history of diabetes, high cholesterol, and peripheral neuropathy. Her most recent A1c in September 2025 was greater than 14%. She was having a pain in her feet. Patient reports that she had previously not been checking her blood sugar consistently or taking her insulin as prescribed. Barriers for not following her treatment plan was explored. Patient reported that she was scared of taking insulin because she had low blood sugars. She was skipping her morning 70/30 insulin and taking her evening dose of insulin 3 hours after her dinner meal. She had expired test strips. Her sugar readings were over 350 consistently. [NOMINEE] explored readiness to engage in improving her health. Patient was motivated. Support and education were provided on how to better manage needed changes. [NOMINEE] addressed barrier with insulin and symptoms management. Education was provided on timing of this insulin in relation to meals. Education was also provided on healthier diet choices, watching portions, and continuing to stay active. [NOMINEE] completed outreach to the provider for an order for a glucometer and the pharmacy to dispense – there was an issue with correct brand of the glucometer due to the patient’s insurance status. Patient received her new glucometer, and assistance was provided on setup and use. Worked with the patient and the pharmacy for timely refill of her insulin and other meds. [NOMINEE]’s outreaches to the patient reinforced diabetes management, following treatment plan, and to address concerns. Over the course of a few weeks, the patient reported doing better. Her blood sugars were improved. She reports that her morning blood sugars were around 140 and bedtime blood sugars were around 160. She states that her neuropathy pain was also somewhat improved. She expressed her appreciation for the support that was provided.

Nomination Reason:
1. 
I am honored to nominate myself, Dr. [NOMINEE], for the Excellence in Community Care Award. Sometimes there is no one who can better tell your story. My work in the ambulatory setting is grounded not only in advanced clinical knowledge and expertise, but in lived experience that has shaped how I care for every patient I encounter. There was a time when I was not the provider—but the person in need. I experienced homelessness while grieving the loss of my mother and supporting my father through illness. In those moments, I understood what it meant to feel unseen, unheard, and uncertain about the future. That experience did not break me—it built me. With unwavering determination, I continued my journey in nursing, earning my credentials as a Family Nurse Practitioner, Psychiatric Mental Health Nurse Practitioner, and Doctor of Nursing Practice. I carried those life experiences with me into every exam room, transforming them into purpose. Today, I provide care with both clinical excellence and deep compassion. I recognize that behind every diagnosis is a story, and I meet my patients where they are—especially those navigating homelessness, mental health challenges, and limited access to care. What I once felt ashamed of has become one of my greatest strengths, allowing me to connect, advocate, and deliver care that is truly patient-centered. In my practice, I consistently demonstrate strong clinical judgment, critical thinking, and a commitment to improving health outcomes. I strive not only to provide exceptional care, but to uplift and mentor colleagues, foster collaboration, and advocate for those whose voices are often unheard. I stand as a reflection of the community I serve—demonstrating that adversity does not define us, but rather strengthens our purpose. Through every patient interaction, I aim to restore not only health, but dignity, trust, and hope. Because for me, community care is not just what I do—it is who I am.
